findings.title: Task.startDate / Task.endDate ordering has no DB-level CHECK constraint
findings.code_evidence:
estimatedHours         Decimal?   @db.Decimal(5, 2) // hours ; DAT-005
  progress               Int        @default(0) // 0-100%
  startDate              DateTime?
  endDate                DateTime?
  startTime              String? // Horaire de début optionnel (format HH:MM)
findings.description: Every other date-bounded entity in the schema has a DB-level `endDate >= startDate` CHECK constraint added via raw SQL migration: projects (`projects_dates_ck`), epics (`epics_dates_ck`), telework_recurring_rules (`telework_recurring_rules_dates_ck`), leave_validation_delegates (`leave_validation_delegates_dates_ck`), school_vacations (`school_vacations_dates_ck`), project_members (`project_members_dates_ck`). The `tasks` table has time-format CHECKs (`tasks_startTime_format_ck`, `tasks_endTime_format_ck`, `tasks_time_order_ck`) but NO corresponding `tasks_dates_ck CHECK (endDate >= startDate)`. A task with `endDate < startDate` can be persisted and will silently produce negative-duration tasks visible on planning views.
findings.root_cause: The migration that added the date ordering constraints (20260527120000_dat003_dat004_business_invariants) covers six models but does not include `tasks`. The subsequent phase4c migration (20260606213229) adds `project_members_dates_ck` but also does not add `tasks_dates_ck`.
findings.impact: A malformed API call or a bulk import can insert a task where `endDate < startDate`. Planning views and analytics would render negative-duration tasks without any DB-level rejection. The gap is consistent across all confirmed migrations.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a raw-SQL migration: `ALTER TABLE "tasks" ADD CONSTRAINT "tasks_dates_ck" CHECK ("endDate" IS NULL OR "startDate" IS NULL OR "endDate" >= "startDate");` (NULL-tolerant, matching the pattern used on epics/leaves/school_vacations).
acceptance_criteria: A migration file exists containing `ADD CONSTRAINT "tasks_dates_ck" CHECK ("endDate" >= "startDate")` (NULL-tolerant variant).
`psql -c "INSERT INTO tasks(..., \"startDate\", \"endDate\", ...) VALUES (..., '2026-07-01', '2026-06-01', ...)"` returns a check_violation error.
Existing tasks with NULL dates are unaffected.

findings.title: Project.budgetHours and Task.estimatedHours lack non-negative DB-level CHECK constraints
findings.code_evidence:
budgetHours     Int?
  icon            String?
  hiddenStatuses  TaskStatus[]  @default([])
findings.description: The schema provides `budgetHours Int?` on Project (line 179) and `estimatedHours Decimal? @db.Decimal(5,2)` on Task (line 325). No migration adds a `CHECK (budgetHours >= 0)` or `CHECK (estimatedHours >= 0)` constraint. The analogous `time_entries_hours_ck` constraint (`CHECK (hours >= 0 AND hours <= 24)`) and `leave_balances_totaldays_ck` (`CHECK (totalDays >= 0)`) show the pattern is known and applied elsewhere. A negative budget or estimated hours value would corrupt analytics and capacity planning.
findings.root_cause: The CHECK constraint sweep in migrations 20260527120000 and 20260528120000 covered leaves/time-entries/subtask position but omitted the planning estimation fields on Project and Task.
findings.impact: Negative budget or estimated hours can be persisted via the API and would corrupt capacity planning, project progress analytics, and workload dashboards. The `Decimal(5,2)` type allows values down to -999.99.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a migration with: `ALTER TABLE "projects" ADD CONSTRAINT "projects_budgetHours_ck" CHECK ("budgetHours" IS NULL OR "budgetHours" >= 0); ALTER TABLE "tasks" ADD CONSTRAINT "tasks_estimatedHours_ck" CHECK ("estimatedHours" IS NULL OR "estimatedHours" >= 0);`
acceptance_criteria: A migration adds non-negative CHECKs for both `projects.budgetHours` and `tasks.estimatedHours`.
Attempting to insert a project with `budgetHours = -1` raises a check_violation.
Attempting to insert a task with `estimatedHours = -0.25` raises a check_violation.

findings.title: setup-cron-backup.sh hard-codes stale local dev path; production backup uses systemd but cron script is diverged
findings.description: The cron setup script hard-codes a developer's local home directory (`/home/alex/Documents/Repository/orchestr-a-refonte`). Per the project MEMORY, the production box has no cron daemon and uses a systemd timer (`backup-daily.timer`) instead. This script would fail silently on prod if ever invoked and would register the wrong script path even on a dev box because the repo is at `/home/alex/Documents/REPO/ORCHESTRA`.
findings.root_cause: The script was written for an earlier project layout and never updated to reflect the VPS production topology or the repo rename.
findings.impact: An operator following this script would install a cron entry pointing at a non-existent path, giving false confidence that automated backups are configured while none actually run.
findings.suggested_fix: Either retire this script (production uses systemd) or update it to use the correct `PROJECT_DIR` and document that it is dev-only.
acceptance_criteria: Script uses the current repo path or is explicitly marked dev-only.
Path is not hard-coded to a developer's home directory.

findings.title: Role.isDefault has no partial unique index to enforce a single default role
findings.code_evidence:
/// Rôle par défaut pour un nouvel utilisateur.
  isDefault Boolean @default(false)

  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
  updatedAt DateTime @updatedAt

  users User[] @relation(name: "UserRoleEntity")

  @@index([templateKey])
  @@index([isDefault])
  @@map("roles")
findings.description: The schema has a B-tree index `@@index([isDefault])` (line 1229) to speed up lookup of the default role. However there is no partial unique index `CREATE UNIQUE INDEX roles_one_default_idx ON roles (isDefault) WHERE isDefault = true`, which means the database permits multiple rows with `isDefault = true`. The application's RBAC bootstrapping logic likely assumes at most one default role; two concurrent role admin operations could silently create an ambiguous state.
findings.root_cause: Prisma PSL cannot express partial unique indexes (`@@unique` with a `WHERE` clause). The convention in this codebase is to add these as raw SQL in migrations (see `leave_balances_global_unique` referenced in a comment on LeaveBalance). No such migration was added for `roles.isDefault`.
findings.impact: Low probability but non-zero: if two admins simultaneously set different roles as default, both can succeed, leaving a permanently ambiguous 'default role' that is resolved non-deterministically at user creation time. The schema comment says 'Rôle par défaut pour un nouvel utilisateur' — exactly the invariant that breaks.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a raw SQL migration: `CREATE UNIQUE INDEX "roles_one_default_idx" ON "roles" ("isDefault") WHERE "isDefault" = true;` (PostgreSQL partial unique index — only one row with `isDefault = true` is permitted).
acceptance_criteria: A migration adds `CREATE UNIQUE INDEX "roles_one_default_idx" ON "roles" ("isDefault") WHERE "isDefault" = true;`.
Attempting to set a second role as `isDefault = true` raises a unique_violation.
Roles with `isDefault = false` (the majority) are unaffected.
findings.notes: Medium confidence because the service layer may enforce single-default via a guard (not checked in this read-only scan); the finding is valid as a DB-level safety net regardless.

findings.title: users.roleId FK uses ON DELETE SET NULL — silent RBAC nullification when a role is deleted
findings.code_evidence: ALTER TABLE "users" ADD CONSTRAINT "users_roleId_fkey" FOREIGN KEY ("roleId") REFERENCES "roles"("id") ON DELETE SET NULL ON UPDATE CASCADE;
findings.description: When a `roles` row is deleted, every user assigned that role has their `roleId` silently set to NULL. Because RBAC V4 resolves permissions compile-time from `ROLE_TEMPLATES[roleCode]`, a NULL roleId returns no permissions for the affected users. This is a silent privilege wipe with no error raised. The schema trend (DAT-007, DAT-022, DAT-028) consistently flipped CASCADE/SET-NULL to RESTRICT on history-sensitive tables — this FK was never revisited.
findings.root_cause: The FK action was set to `ON DELETE SET NULL` at RBAC V0 introduction and was not tightened when V4 made roleId the sole permission source. No later migration converts it to RESTRICT or NO ACTION. `schema.prisma` confirms `roleId String?` remains nullable.
findings.impact: Deleting any non-system role (possible for ADMIN) silently nullifies permissions for all users assigned to it. Affected users can still authenticate but receive zero permissions. No DB error is raised, so the event may go undetected until a user reports access loss.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the FK to `ON DELETE RESTRICT`. Deleting a role while users are assigned to it should be a hard error at the DB layer, forcing an explicit reassignment step. If soft-delete of roles is desired, add a `deletedAt` column to `roles` and use a partial index on `roleId WHERE deletedAt IS NULL`.
acceptance_criteria: A new migration drops the `users_roleId_fkey` constraint and recreates it with `ON DELETE RESTRICT`.
The application's role-deletion endpoint either reassigns users first or raises a 409 when assignees exist.
An integration test verifies that DELETE /roles/:id with active assignees returns 409, not 200.
findings.verification_command: psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT rc.constraint_name, rc.delete_rule FROM information_schema.referential_constraints rc JOIN information_schema.key_column_usage kcu ON rc.constraint_name = kcu.constraint_name WHERE kcu.table_name = 'users' AND kcu.column_name = 'roleId';"
findings.notes: Schema.prisma confirms roleId remains String? (nullable). This is distinct from the 'roleId never made NOT NULL' observation — that is an intentional transition state per the migration comment. The ON DELETE SET NULL behavior is the actual integrity gap.

findings.title: DAT-021 migration breaks existing audit_log row hash chain until a manual recompute script is run
findings.code_evidence:
--   computeRowHash now folds schemaVersion into the canonical concat. Backfilling
--   schemaVersion=1 therefore changes every existing row's hash input → every
--   rowHash must be recomputed. That walk runs AFTER this migration via
--   scripts/recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts (advisory-lock + trigger-disable
--   inside one transaction, SYSTEM_BACKFILL-bracketed). See the runbook in the
--   DAT-021 closing commit body. Until the recompute runs, freshly inserted rows
--   are self-consistent (they hash with schemaVersion); pre-existing rows verify
--   only after the recompute.
findings.description: The migration adds `schemaVersion INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1` to `audit_logs` and backfills it atomically. However, `computeRowHash` now includes `schemaVersion` in its hash input, which changes the expected hash for every pre-existing row. The migration itself does NOT recompute hashes — it explicitly defers this to `scripts/recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts`. Between `prisma migrate deploy` and the manual script execution, any hash-chain verification run against pre-DAT-021 rows will fail.
findings.root_cause: The recompute step was deliberately decoupled from the migration (the migration comment references the runbook in the DAT-021 closing commit). While this is a conscious design decision, the decoupling creates an operational window where the audit chain is unverifiable. There is no migration-time guard (e.g., a DO block that errors if the recompute is not complete) to enforce sequencing.
findings.impact: During the window between migration deployment and recompute script execution, any tamper-detection audit that walks the hash chain for pre-DAT-021 rows will produce false positives (every row appears tampered). For a French local government HR system, this could trigger unnecessary incident response. It is also undetectable from the migration log alone whether the recompute was actually run in production.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a post-migration verification step in the deployment runbook: `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM audit_logs WHERE rowHash != computeRowHash(...)` — if non-zero, block the deployment signoff. Alternatively, add a boolean `hashRecomputedAt` column or an entries in a `schema_maintenance` table that the recompute script populates on completion, making the status auditable.
acceptance_criteria: The recompute script `apps/api/src/scripts/recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts` has been executed against prod (verifiable via the advisory-lock log or a sentinel DB record).
Hash-chain verification query returns 0 mismatches for rows with `schemaVersion = 1`.
Deployment runbook or CI post-migrate step documents when the recompute must run relative to `prisma migrate deploy`.
findings.verification_command: psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM audit_logs WHERE \"schemaVersion\" = 1 AND \"rowHash\" IS NOT NULL" # non-zero means rows exist; run scripts/recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts if it was not confirmed executed
findings.notes: The script exists at apps/api/src/scripts/recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts (confirmed in git at 33f7a9ce). The concern is strictly operational: whether the script was actually run in prod. The git log does not confirm execution. The MEMORY.md prod deploy log does not mention it. Medium confidence because there may be a runbook entry or post-deploy hook not visible in the repo.

findings.title: backup-database.sh: no encryption, no offsite transfer, no integrity verification
findings.code_evidence:
docker exec "${CONTAINER_NAME}" pg_dump -U "${DATABASE_USER}" "${DATABASE_NAME}" > "${BACKUP_DIR}/${BACKUP_FILE}"

# Compresser la sauvegarde
gzip "${BACKUP_DIR}/${BACKUP_FILE}"
findings.description: The backup script writes plain-text SQL dumps compressed with gzip only (no encryption). Backups are stored at a relative path (`./backups`) on the same host. There is no offsite transfer (S3, rsync, scp), no WORM destination, and no minimum-size or integrity check (e.g. `gunzip -t`) after compression. A dump that fails mid-stream still produces a partial file that passes the existence check on line 31 (`if [ -f ... ]`).
findings.root_cause: The script was written as a simple operational shortcut and was never hardened for production data-protection requirements. The `set -e` flag catches docker exec failures but not a partial dump that exits 0.
findings.impact: A successful attacker or accidental host-disk failure destroys both the live DB and the only backup copy simultaneously. A corrupt partial dump is not detected until a restore is attempted. Personal data (audit trail, leave history) is unprotected at rest in the backup file.
findings.suggested_fix: 1. Pipe through `openssl enc` or `age` before writing to disk. 2. After compression, run `gunzip -t` to verify gzip integrity before declaring success. 3. Add `set -o pipefail` so a mid-stream pg_dump failure propagates. 4. Add a minimum-size check (`[ $(stat -c%s ...) -gt $MIN_BYTES ]`). 5. rsync/s3 the encrypted archive to an offsite location. 6. Periodically test restores.
acceptance_criteria: Backups are encrypted at rest (key not stored alongside the backup).
Script uses `set -o pipefail` so a failing pg_dump aborts the script.
Integrity of each archive is verified with `gunzip -t` before the script exits 0.
At least one offsite copy is made after each successful backup.
A minimum-size threshold rejects suspiciously small dumps.

findings.title: RBAC V4 legacy DROP migration lacks inline data backup; only a comment instructs operator to take a backup beforehand
findings.description: This migration irreversibly drops three tables (`role_permissions`, `permissions`, `role_configs`) and a column (`users.role`) plus an enum type. The only safety net is a comment advising the operator to take a backup. There is no inline `CREATE TABLE ... AS SELECT` snapshot (as the DAT-005 pattern demonstrates), no rollback script, and no preflight check script. If the migration applies on a database where `prisma migrate deploy` was not preceded by a backup, the data is permanently lost.
findings.root_cause: The DAT-005 backup-before-drop pattern (snapshot table + preflight script + rollback SQL) was developed after this migration was written. This older migration pre-dates that practice.
findings.impact: If applied on production without a prior backup, all RBAC permission configuration data is permanently lost with no recovery path. Prisma `migrate deploy` is atomic per-migration but cannot roll back a DROP TABLE after commit.
findings.suggested_fix: This migration has already been applied to production, so backfilling a rollback script is moot. Document this in the migration or in a CHANGELOG entry. For future DROP migrations, require the DAT-005 preflight+snapshot+rollback pattern.
acceptance_criteria: A post-mortem note or code comment confirms whether prod data was preserved.
A project-level convention document requires preflight+snapshot for all future DROP migrations.
findings.verification_command: N/A — migration already applied; this is a historical finding

findings.title: project_snapshots missing cross-column CHECK: tasksDone <= tasksTotal
findings.description: The constraint added by DAT-008/015 only enforces that both counts are non-negative. It does not prevent the business-impossible state where `tasksDone > tasksTotal` (more tasks done than total tasks). This invariant is enforced by application logic but not at the DB layer.
findings.root_cause: The initial CHECK was scoped to the simplest non-negative guard. No subsequent migration adds the cross-column comparison. Checklist item 3 (missing CHECKs that COULD exist but do not) surfaces this gap.
findings.impact: A faulty snapshot computation, direct DB write, or future service bug could produce rows where `tasksDone > tasksTotal`. Downstream analytics (dashboard KPIs, milestone progress bars) would display progress > 100%. Low risk because snapshots are system-generated, but the DB provides no floor.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single-migration CHECK: `ALTER TABLE "project_snapshots" ADD CONSTRAINT "project_snapshots_task_counts_cross_ck" CHECK ("tasksDone" <= "tasksTotal");`
acceptance_criteria: A migration adds `CHECK ("tasksDone" <= "tasksTotal")` to `project_snapshots`.
A unit test or integration test verifies that inserting a snapshot with tasksDone > tasksTotal raises a DB error.
findings.notes: project_snapshots is an analytics/read table generated by a scheduled job, not by direct user input, so exploitation risk is very low. The finding is real but the practical impact is limited.

findings.title: recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts: STARTED SYSTEM_BACKFILL row is emitted before the recompute transaction, then re-hashed inside it — the emitted row's hash is stale after recompute
findings.description: The STARTED row is inserted with a valid chain hash (prevHash = prior tail, rowHash = sha256 of that row). The recompute then walks from genesis and rehashes every row including the STARTED row. The COMPLETED row is inserted after the recompute with the correct new prevHash. This is functionally correct (the code comment says so) but means the STARTED row's `rowHash` as originally computed by `emitSystemBackfill` is discarded and overwritten. An external verifier inspecting the row immediately after the STARTED insert (but before the recompute commits) would see a hash that is valid at that instant but will be overwritten. Not a bug, but creates a window where the trail is temporarily inconsistent.
findings.root_cause: The design choice to emit STARTED before the recompute transaction is intentional (it brackets the operation in the trail) but the STARTED row is inevitably rehashed by the walk.
findings.impact: Minimal: only relevant during the narrow window of the recompute transaction. A monitoring tool that reads audit_logs during the recompute could see a row whose hash was recomputed (changed). No data is permanently wrong.
findings.suggested_fix: Consider emitting STARTED inside the recompute transaction (after the trigger disable but before the walk) to make the STARTED row's position in the chain deterministic. Alternatively, document this ordering clearly in a code comment (it is already partially documented).
acceptance_criteria: Code comment clearly explains that STARTED is re-hashed by the recompute and why this is acceptable.

findings.title: leave_no_overlap EXCLUDE covers only APPROVED status — CANCELLATION_REQUESTED overlap is app-only
findings.code_evidence:
ALTER TABLE "leaves"
  ADD CONSTRAINT "leaves_no_overlap"
  EXCLUDE USING gist (
    "userId" WITH =,
    daterange("startDate", "endDate", '[]') WITH &&
  )
  WHERE (status = 'APPROVED');
findings.description: The EXCLUDE constraint intentionally scopes to `status = 'APPROVED'` only. The migration comment (lines 20-24) explains this is deliberate: only the transition `PENDING → APPROVED` is the auditable race path the DB must guard. CANCELLATION_REQUESTED overlap is handled at the app layer by `checkOverlap()` in `leaves.service.ts` which includes CANCELLATION_REQUESTED in its status filter. This means the DB floor is narrower than the app's own overlap semantics: a direct SQL INSERT with status CANCELLATION_REQUESTED that overlaps an existing CANCELLATION_REQUESTED leave would not be rejected by the DB.
findings.root_cause: Intentional design trade-off documented in the migration comment. The migration author chose not to include CANCELLATION_REQUESTED in the partial WHERE because it is a transitional state that does not represent a committed leave.
findings.impact: Very low in practice — CANCELLATION_REQUESTED leaves cannot be created by direct SQL in normal operation, and the app layer check is in place. The gap matters only if a bulk-import or admin tool bypasses the service layer.
findings.suggested_fix: No immediate action required. If a bulk import tool is ever added, ensure it calls the service layer's checkOverlap() or extends the DB EXCLUDE to cover CANCELLATION_REQUESTED status as well.
acceptance_criteria: Document the intentional APPROVED-only scope in the ADR or migration comment (already done in the migration; no action needed).
Any future bulk-import tool must invoke service-layer overlap checks.
findings.verification_command: psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT indexname, indexdef FROM pg_indexes WHERE tablename = 'leaves' AND indexname = 'leaves_no_overlap';"
findings.notes: The migration comment explicitly addresses this design choice. Flagged per checklist item 2 (ON DELETE / ON UPDATE cascade semantics and DB floor completeness) but the intentional design is well-documented. Lowest severity — included for completeness only.

findings.title: Leave.leaveTypeId FK has no onDelete in Prisma schema (defaults to NoAction/error in Prisma 5+), but SQL migration shows RESTRICT — inconsistency risks blocking leave-type archival
findings.description: The `Leave.leaveType` relation carries no explicit `onDelete` in the Prisma PSL. In Prisma 6 with a required (non-nullable) FK, the default is `Restrict`. The migration SQL confirms RESTRICT (`ON DELETE RESTRICT`). `LeaveBalance.leaveType` correctly documents `onDelete: Restrict` explicitly. The asymmetry between the two relations is a documentation/clarity gap. More importantly, RESTRICT means an admin cannot archive (delete) a `LeaveTypeConfig` that has any leave row referencing it, even historical ones — this could silently block administrative operations without a clear application-layer message.
findings.root_cause: The FK onDelete was not specified when the Prisma model was written; the implicit Prisma default matches what the SQL migration installs, but the omission is a maintenance risk when reading the schema.
findings.impact: Attempting to delete a `LeaveTypeConfig` that is referenced by any `Leave` row will raise a Prisma P2003 error without a typed application-layer guard, surfacing a raw database error to the operator.
findings.suggested_fix: Add explicit `onDelete: Restrict` to the `Leave.leaveType` relation so intent is documented. Add an application-layer ConflictException guard in `LeaveTypeService.delete()` that lists referencing leave rows before attempting the DB delete.
acceptance_criteria: Leave.leaveType has explicit `onDelete: Restrict` in schema.prisma.
LeaveTypeService delete operation gives a typed error (not raw P2003) when leaves exist.

findings.title: LeaveBalance.year has no CHECK constraint to reject obviously invalid years
findings.description: The `LeaveBalance.year` column stores a calendar year (e.g. 2024). No DB-level CHECK constraint prevents a year of 0, -1, or 9999. A data entry error or a service-layer bug could store an invalid year that would be invisible until a query groups by year and surfaces an anomalous bucket.
findings.root_cause: Year columns are typically low-risk, and the DAT-004 family focused on numeric magnitude columns. Year range was not included.
findings.impact: Low: mostly a data quality concern. An invalid year on a leave balance row could cause incorrect balance calculations or confusing display.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: `ALTER TABLE "leave_balances" ADD CONSTRAINT "leave_balances_year_ck" CHECK ("year" >= 2000 AND "year" <= 2100);`
acceptance_criteria: A DB-level CHECK constraint rejects leave_balances rows with year outside a reasonable range.
findings.verification_command: docker exec orchestr-a-db psql -U postgres -d orchestr_a_v2_e2e -c "SELECT conname FROM pg_constraint WHERE conrelid = 'leave_balances'::regclass AND contype = 'c';"

findings.title: Leave.days Decimal(6,2) allows values up to 9999.99 — a single leave row spanning 9999 days is logically impossible but not constrained
findings.code_evidence: days        Decimal     @db.Decimal(6, 2) // Jours ouvrés calculés ; DAT-005 (max 9999.99)
findings.description: The `days` column on `Leave` has a `CHECK (days > 0)` constraint (from DAT-004) but no upper bound. A single leave row of 9999.99 days (~27 years) is technically possible via a direct DB write. The service-layer calculation floors at 0.5 days and computes working days from date range, so the app will never create such a row, but there is no DB-level ceiling.
findings.root_cause: The DAT-004 migration added only a lower bound (`> 0`) for Leave.days and did not add an upper bound.
findings.impact: Very low in practice. A corrupt leave row with an enormous `days` value could skew leave-balance analytics but would only occur via a direct DB write or a severe service-layer bug.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an upper bound: `ALTER TABLE "leaves" ADD CONSTRAINT "leaves_days_upper_ck" CHECK ("days" <= 365);` (one year of working days is around 260; 365 is a generous ceiling).
acceptance_criteria: An upper bound CHECK on leaves.days is present in the DB schema.
findings.verification_command: docker exec orchestr-a-db psql -U postgres -d orchestr_a_v2_e2e -c "SELECT conname, pg_get_constraintdef(oid) FROM pg_constraint WHERE conrelid = 'leaves'::regclass AND contype = 'c';"

verified_clean: No Float type anywhere in schema.prisma — every money/time/balance/progress field uses Decimal(@db.Decimal) or Int. Checklist item 4 is clean. Verified by: grep -n 'Float' schema.prisma returns empty.

audit_logs immutability trigger (OBS-002): trigger function `audit_logs_immutable()` and `BEFORE UPDATE OR DELETE` trigger `audit_logs_no_update_delete` confirmed in migration 20260525190000_audit_logs_immutability_hash_chain_actor_snapshot. The schema comment is accurate.
audit_logs.actorId FK uses ON DELETE NO ACTION / ON UPDATE NO ACTION (not SetNull) to prevent the trigger-blocked SET NULL from firing. Confirmed in schema line 1251 and migration 20260606213229.
TimeEntry.hours CHECK constraint: `time_entries_hours_ck CHECK (hours >= 0 AND hours <= 24)` confirmed in migration 20260528120000.
Task.progress 0-100 CHECK: `tasks_progress_ck CHECK (progress BETWEEN 0 AND 100)` confirmed in migration 20260527120000.
Epic.progress 0-100 CHECK: `epics_progress_ck CHECK (progress BETWEEN 0 AND 100)` confirmed in migration 20260527120000.
ProjectMember.allocation 0-100 CHECK: confirmed in migration 20260605192741 and 20260606213229.
PredefinedTask.weight 1-5 CHECK: `predefined_tasks_weight_ck CHECK (weight BETWEEN 1 AND 5)` confirmed in migration 20260527120000.

startDate <= endDate CHECKs: confirmed for projects, epics, leaves, telework_recurring_rules, leave_validation_delegates, school_vacations, project_members, events recurrence — all via migration 20260527120000 and 20260606213229.

dayOfWeek 0-6 CHECK: confirmed for both `events.recurrenceDay` (20260606213229) and `telework_recurring_rules.dayOfWeek` (20260606213229) and `predefined_task_recurring_rules.dayOfWeek` (20260605201203).
Recurrence type/field coherence CHECK (WEEKLY→dayOfWeek, MONTHLY_DAY→monthlyDayOfMonth, MONTHLY_ORDINAL→monthlyOrdinal+dayOfWeek): confirmed in migration 20260605201203.
monthlyOrdinal 1-5 and monthlyDayOfMonth 1-31 range CHECKs: confirmed in migration 20260605201203.
Time format HH:MM regex CHECKs on Task, Event, PredefinedTask startTime/endTime: confirmed in migration 20260527140000.
Time ordering (startTime <= endTime) CHECKs with lpad zero-padding: confirmed in migration 20260606213229.
Task self-dependency prevention CHECK (taskId <> dependsOnTaskId) and cycle-prevention trigger: confirmed in migration 20260527180000.
TimeEntry actor XOR constraint (userId XOR thirdPartyId, not both NULL): confirmed in migration 20260411100717.
Document.contentSha256 format CHECK (64-char lowercase hex or NULL): confirmed in migration 20260606213229.
documents.size >= 0 CHECK: confirmed in migration 20260527120000.
subtasks.position >= 0 CHECK: confirmed in migration 20260528120000.
Leave.days > 0 CHECK and leave_balances.totalDays >= 0 CHECK: confirmed in migration 20260527120000.

Two-role DB split (runtime restricted role + DDL owner role): documented in schema datasource comment and prisma/init-roles.sql. The REVOKE UPDATE/DELETE/TRUNCATE on audit_logs for the app role is a documented defence-in-depth layer.
ProjectSnapshot.progress uses Decimal(5,2) not Float — DAT-005 fix confirmed.
LeaveBalance.totalDays and Leave.days both use Decimal — DAT-005 clean.
LeaveTypeConfig delete blocked by Restrict on LeaveBalance.leaveTypeId — DAT-004 confirmed.
ProjectSnapshot.onDelete: Restrict on projectId — history preserved on project hard-delete, confirmed line 229.
Document.onDelete: Restrict on projectId — documents preserved on project hard-delete, confirmed line 818.
TimeEntry ThirdParty Restrict and Project Restrict — billing time preserved, confirmed lines 473, 475.
LeaveBalance userId unique composite [userId, leaveTypeId, year] confirmed — prevents duplicate accrual rows.
RBAC V4: permissions are compile-time via templateKey, no DB role_permissions table in current schema — consistent with MEMORY notes.
Deployment model has no user FK (frozen `deployedBy` string) — RGPD snapshot rationale consistent with AuditLog pattern.

Float-to-Decimal conversion (DAT-005): Two-migration split pattern (20260524100000 backup + 20260524100100 convert) ensures backup tables survive atomically before conversion. Backup tables later dropped cleanly (20260603120000). leaves.days, time_entries.hours, leave_balances.totalDays, tasks.estimatedHours, project_snapshots.progress all converted to NUMERIC with correct precision.

audit_logs row-level immutability trigger: 20260525190000 creates audit_logs_immutable() BEFORE UPDATE OR DELETE trigger that raises on any modification attempt. Verified in migration SQL — no bypass path via UPDATE or DELETE.

audit_logs TRUNCATE protection: 20260605193013 adds BEFORE TRUNCATE statement-level trigger reusing audit_logs_immutable(). Closes the gap where TRUNCATE bypassed the row-level trigger. Both edges now covered.

audit_logs.actorId ON UPDATE asymmetry fixed: 20260606213229 (DAT-028) corrects the FK from ON UPDATE CASCADE to ON UPDATE NO ACTION, preventing a users.id rotation from cascade-mutating immutable audit rows.

Project history preservation via RESTRICT FKs: 20260525200000 (DAT-007) converts tasks, project_snapshots, documents, time_entries projectId FKs from CASCADE/SET-NULL to RESTRICT. Hard-deleting a project with any child records raises a DB error — history is preserved by force.

Audit actor snapshot preservation: 20260525190000 changes audit_logs.actorId FK from ON DELETE SET NULL to ON DELETE NO ACTION. User hard-delete is handled at app layer (users.service.ts hardDelete() anonymises the shell row) — the FK correctly prevents silent nullification of immutable audit rows.

Task dependency cycle prevention: 20260527180000 (DAT-018) adds a BEFORE INSERT/UPDATE trigger using a recursive CTE to detect cycles before they are committed. A valid self-referential insert raises immediately.
Event parent cycle prevention: 20260528140000 (DAT-038) adds equivalent cycle-detection trigger for events.parentId. Mirrors DAT-018 pattern.

Task-project cross-table consistency: 20260528150000 (DAT-037) adds a trigger enforcing that a task's parentTaskId.projectId matches the task's own projectId, and propagates projectId to child tasks when a parent changes. Prevents orphaned subtask references.

RBAC V4 irreversibility is explicitly documented: 20260420120000 carries the comment '-- ACTION IRREVERSIBLE — Assurer un backup avant déploiement prod.' The tables dropped (role_permissions, permissions, role_configs) are genuinely obsolete after compile-time ROLE_TEMPLATES resolution. Intentional, documented, not a gap.

Leave balance global unique index: 20260523171000 adds UNIQUE index on (userId, leaveTypeId) WHERE userId IS NULL for global balances, and on (userId, leaveTypeId) WHERE userId IS NOT NULL for per-user balances. Both partial indexes correctly prevent duplicate balance records for the same (user, type) pair.

Functional unique indexes for case-insensitive uniqueness: 20260603140000 (DAT-015) adds CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ON users (LOWER(email)) and LOWER(login). Prevents case-variant duplicate accounts without requiring application-layer normalization.

Enum backfill assertion in RBAC V0: 20260419192835 backfills roleId from legacy role enum and asserts via RAISE EXCEPTION that no users remain with NULL roleId post-backfill. The migration cannot complete if the backfill is partial.

leave_type autosync trigger: 20260527150000 (DAT-014) adds a trigger that propagates leave_type name/color changes to leave_balances. Keeps denormalized display fields consistent without relying on application layer.

Document FK soft-delete pattern: 20260603214608 (DAT-025) sets documents.projectId and documents.taskId FKs to ON DELETE SET NULL rather than CASCADE, preserving uploaded documents when their parent project/task is deleted. Intentional archival posture.

Department RESTRICT FK: 20260603215133 (DAT-022) converts services.departmentId FK from CASCADE to RESTRICT. Deleting a department that still has services raises a DB error — data hierarchy is enforced.

Time-format CHECKs with lpad zero-padding: 20260606213229 uses lpad(endTime, 5, '0') >= lpad(startTime, 5, '0') to correctly order single-digit hour times ('9:05' vs '17:00'). The comment explicitly addresses the lexicographic trap and applies the fix to tasks, events, and predefined_tasks.

Recurrence field XOR invariant: 20260605201203 (DAT-011) adds a CHECK that enforces the correct field combination per recurrenceType (WEEKLY/MONTHLY_DAY/MONTHLY_ORDINAL), mirroring the DTO validation. Raw inserts with partial recurrence fields are rejected.
project_members date ordering CHECK: 20260606213229 adds CHECK (endDate IS NULL OR startDate IS NULL OR endDate >= startDate) with correct NULL-tolerance.
documents.contentSha256 format CHECK: 20260606213229 adds CHECK matching '^[0-9a-f]{64}$' for SHA-256 format validation. NULL-tolerant for documents without a hash.
Snapshot projectId uniqueness per date: 20260603130000 (COR-014) adds UNIQUE (projectId, date) on project_snapshots. Prevents duplicate daily snapshots for the same project.
Password reset token indexed: 20260604050007 (DAT-028) adds unique index on password_reset_tokens.token and TTL-supporting index on expiresAt. Token lookup is O(log n) and expiry sweeps are indexed.

User soft-delete shell pattern: 20260604110510 (DAT-026) adds users.deletedAt timestamp. Combined with users.service.ts hardDelete() which anonymises email/login to tombstone values, the pattern preserves audit_logs FK integrity without violating GDPR erasure intent.

All 87 migration files were read and examined. No migration uses a bare DDL statement outside an implicit transaction that could leave the schema in a partial state — every migration file contains only DDL that PostgreSQL wraps atomically by default.

btree_gist extension for GiST EXCLUDE: The EXCLUDE constraint in 20260527190000 requires the btree_gist extension. The init migration (20251116093059) does not enable it; the extension is expected to be present in the PostgreSQL instance. This is standard practice for Prisma + Docker Compose deployments where the extension is enabled at cluster init time — verified acceptable.

audit_logs immutability: BEFORE UPDATE/DELETE trigger (audit_logs_no_update_delete) verified present and tested in audit-immutability.int.spec.ts; trigger function raises check_violation (SQLSTATE 23514)
audit_logs TRUNCATE protection: separate BEFORE TRUNCATE statement-level trigger (audit_logs_no_truncate) added in migration 20260605193013, tested in audit-immutability.int.spec.ts DAT-013 case

Privilege-layer defence-in-depth: app_user role has UPDATE/DELETE/TRUNCATE REVOKEd on audit_logs via init-roles.sql; independently witnessed in audit-role-revoke.int.spec.ts including trigger-disabled scenario
audit_logs.actorId FK: ON DELETE NO ACTION AND ON UPDATE NO ACTION (migration 20260606213229); prevents cascading mutations on immutable rows; final state confirmed in schema.prisma line 1251

Hash chain implementation: computeRowHash uses sha256, stable key-sorted JSON serialization, includes all identity fields (action|entityType|entityId|actorId|schemaVersion|createdAt|payload|prevHash); no truncation

Advisory lock serialisation: pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext('audit_logs_chain')) acquired inside every INSERT transaction to enforce a total order on the chain; used consistently in both audit-persistence.service.ts and recompute scripts

Payload schema validation: exhaustive Zod registry (AUDIT_PAYLOAD_SCHEMAS) keyed on all AuditAction members with .strict() top-level schemas; validated before DB insert in validatePayloadForAction; compile-time exhaustiveness via satisfies Record<AuditAction, z.ZodTypeAny>

Float-to-Decimal migration: all money/time/balance columns converted from Float to Decimal — time_entries.hours (Decimal(5,2)), leaves.days (Decimal(6,2)), leave_balances.totalDays (Decimal(6,2)), tasks.estimatedHours (Decimal(5,2)), project_snapshots.progress (Decimal(5,2)); preflight script and rollback SQL exist
DAT-005 backup tables properly dropped: migration 20260603120000 drops the Float backup tables with IF EXISTS; idempotent and removes Prisma schema drift

date-range CHECKs: leaves, projects, epics, telework_recurring_rules, leave_validation_delegates, school_vacations, events.recurrenceEndDate all have endDate >= startDate constraints (migration 20260527120000)
Leave overlap: EXCLUDE USING gist constraint with btree_gist prevents concurrent overlapping APPROVED leaves for same user (migration 20260527190000); closes TOCTOU race at DB level

Numeric bounds CHECKs: leave_balances.totalDays >= 0, leaves.days > 0, tasks.progress 0-100, epics.progress 0-100, predefined_tasks.weight 1-5, project_members.allocation 0-100, documents.size >= 0 (migration 20260527120000), time_entries.hours 0-24, subtasks.position >= 0 (migration 20260528120000), project_snapshots.progress and task counts (migration 20260605192741)
project_third_party_members.allocation 0-100 or NULL CHECK present (migration 20260605192741)
tasks.startTime/endTime ordering CHECK using lpad for correct lexicographic comparison of HH:MM (migration 20260606213229)
events.recurrenceDay 0-6 CHECK and recurrenceWeekInterval >= 1 CHECK present (migration 20260606213229)
telework_recurring_rules.dayOfWeek 0-6 CHECK present (migration 20260606213229)
predefined_task_recurring_rules: XOR recurrence-field CHECKs (WEEKLY/MONTHLY_DAY/MONTHLY_ORDINAL field requirements) and range bounds present (migration 20260605201203)
documents.contentSha256 format CHECK: 64-char lowercase hex or NULL (migration 20260606213229)
project_members dates ordering CHECK: endDate >= startDate or NULL (migration 20260606213229)
ProjectSnapshot FK: ON DELETE RESTRICT (not Cascade) preserves progress history when project is hard-deleted (migration 20260525200000 + schema.prisma line 229)
tasks.projectId FK: ON DELETE RESTRICT prevents project hard-delete when tasks exist; application-layer guard (checkProjectDependencies) surfaces ConflictException before Prisma P2003
time_entries.projectId FK: ON DELETE RESTRICT (not SetNull) preserves time-entry project link on hard-delete (migration 20260525200000)
documents.projectId FK: ON DELETE RESTRICT (not Cascade) preserves documents on project hard-delete (migration 20260525200000)
time_entries.thirdPartyId FK: ON DELETE RESTRICT (not Cascade) prevents third-party deletion when time entries reference it; protects billing history (migration 20260605182729)
leave_balances.leaveTypeId FK: ON DELETE RESTRICT prevents leave type deletion when balance history exists (migration 20260605182729)
leaves.leaveTypeId FK: ON DELETE RESTRICT; verified in migration 20251215120000; Prisma PSL default for required non-nullable relation also resolves to Restrict
audit_logs.rowHash NOT NULL enforced via ALTER COLUMN after backfill (migration 20260525190000); new rows always have hash via computeRowHash
schemaVersion column: NOT NULL DEFAULT 1, added with zero-downtime PG catalog default (no table rewrite); folded into computeRowHash so version drift changes rowHash (migration 20260526120000)
DAT-014 autosync trigger: leaves_sync_type_trg keeps legacy Leave.type column in sync with leaveTypeId automatically on every INSERT/UPDATE, eliminating dual-write drift

Chain recompute scripts: both normalize-action-codes.ts and recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts acquire advisory lock, disable trigger inside transaction (transactional DDL), re-enable trigger, run in-tx verification, then assert predecessor row was not modified
RBAC V4 drop-legacy migration has no backup for the three dropped tables — documented as pre-DAT-005 pattern; already applied to production so rollback is moot
recompute-chain.ts: shared primitive imported from write path (computeRowHash never reimplemented); predecessor-untouched assertion guards against off-by-one
AuditAction enum: exhaustive Record used in ENTITY_TYPE_BY_ACTION and AUDIT_PAYLOAD_SCHEMAS; compile-time witnesses in audit-action.compile-witness.ts and audit-payload-registry.compile-witness.ts

drop_max_days_per_year migration: preflight script (preflight-drop-max-days-per-year.sh) creates committed backup table before migration; rollback SQL script (rollback-drop-max-days-per-year.sql) with sanity count check exists
departmentId FK on User: ON DELETE RESTRICT (migration 20260603215133) prevents department deletion when users are assigned to it

unique indexes: Client.name globally unique; Service.name unique per department; ProjectSnapshot.projectId+date unique; LeaveBalance.userId+leaveTypeId+year unique; global LeaveBalance partial unique index for userId IS NULL
GIN index on audit_logs.payload (jsonb_path_ops) and btree on schemaVersion present for query performance (migration 20260526120000)
Composite descending index on audit_logs(createdAt DESC, id DESC) for chain-tip lookup performance (migration 20260606213229)
